title: Work, Energy, Power and Collisions
subject: Physics
content:
# Work, Energy, Power and Collisions

## Quick Synopsis

Energy methods bypass force-time details when forces are conservative or work is easier to compute. Collisions require momentum first, energy only when elastic.

## Must Know

- Work is force component along displacement integrated over displacement.
- Conservative forces have path-independent work and associated potential energy.
- Work-energy theorem works for all net work, including friction.
- Mechanical energy is conserved only when non-conservative work is zero.
- Power is rate of doing work or F · v.
- In collisions, linear momentum is conserved if external impulse is negligible.

## Formulas / Rules

- W = ∫ F · ds; for constant force W = Fs cosθ.
- K = 1/2 mv²; gravitational U = mgh near Earth; spring U = 1/2 kx².
- Work-energy theorem: Wnet = ΔK.
- Power: P = dW/dt = F · v.
- Coefficient of restitution: e = relative speed of separation / relative speed of approach.

## JEE Focus

- Use energy for springs, variable forces, vertical circles, and smooth tracks.
- Use impulse-momentum for explosions and collisions.
- For rough surfaces, include work done by friction as negative unless friction assists motion.
- In elastic collision of equal masses in 1D, velocities exchange.

## Common Traps

- Momentum conservation does not imply kinetic energy conservation.
- Normal force may do work if point of contact moves, as in wedge problems.
- Friction can convert mechanical energy into heat; it is not always μmg times total distance unless N is constant.
- Potential energy reference level is arbitrary; differences matter.

## 10-Minute Revision Checklist

- Can you identify conservative and non-conservative forces?
- Can you solve spring-block energy problems?
- Can you apply coefficient of restitution?
- Can you distinguish impulse from force?
